coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted significant attention due to their unique electronic, optical, physical, and chemical properties. As nanoscale fragments of graphene rich in electrons, GQDs offer enhanced capabilities that elevate their potential across a wide range of applications. This review paper delves into the synthesis, characterization, and applications of functionalized graphene quantum dots (FGQDs), which exhibit exceptional photoelectronic properties resulting from quantum confinement and edge effects. These features position FGQDs as promising materials for optoelectronic technologies. The review also highlights various functionalization strategies, providing valuable insights for researchers seeking to optimize GQDs for specific applications. By understanding the correct functionalization techniques, researchers can tailor the properties of FGQDs to enhance their performance in a range of applications. This article offers an in-depth discussion of synthetic approaches for producing FGQDs with diverse chemical groups and functionalities, alongside a thorough overview of characterization techniques, ranging from morphological and crystallographic analysis to componential and absorption spectroscopy. Furthermore, the review explores the growing potential of FGQDs in biomedical applications, including biosensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, and therapeutics. It underscores the advances in research and development that are crucial for unlocking the full biomedical potential of FGQDs, while also addressing the challenges that remain to be tackled in the future. As the field continues to evolve, the insights presented in this review provide a solid foundation for future breakthroughs in the synthesis and application of FGQDs.

head.ce:title: Functionalized Graphene Quantum Dots (FGQDs): A review of their synthesis, properties, and emerging biomedical applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted significant attention due to their unique electronic, optical, physical, and chemical properties. As nanoscale fragments of graphene rich in electrons, GQDs offer enhanced capabilities that elevate their potential across a wide range of applications. This review paper delves into the synthesis, characterization, and applications of functionalized graphene quantum dots (FGQDs), which exhibit exceptional photoelectronic properties resulting from quantum confinement and edge effects. These features position FGQDs as promising materials for optoelectronic technologies. The review also highlights various functionalization strategies, providing valuable insights for researchers seeking to optimize GQDs for specific applications. By understanding the correct functionalization techniques, researchers can tailor the properties of FGQDs to enhance their performance in a range of applications. This article offers an in-depth discussion of synthetic approaches for producing FGQDs with diverse chemical groups and functionalities, alongside a thorough overview of characterization techniques, ranging from morphological and crystallographic analysis to componential and absorption spectroscopy. Furthermore, the review explores the growing potential of FGQDs in biomedical applications, including biosensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, and therapeutics. It underscores the advances in research and development that are crucial for unlocking the full biomedical potential of FGQDs, while also addressing the challenges that remain to be tackled in the future. As the field continues to evolve, the insights presented in this review provide a solid foundation for future breakthroughs in the synthesis and application of FGQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the nanostructured metal/carbon based catalysts have been reported as potential advanced candidates for various emerging technological applications [1–6]. Among the catalyst materials, graphene is a carbon allotrope characterized by a honeycomb-like lattice, where each carbon atom is tightly bonded to its neighbors. Its fundamental structure consists of a zero-band gap semiconductor, as there is minimal overlap between the valence and conduction bands [7–11]. Graphene exhibits exceptional properties, including high charge carrier mobility, flexibility, mechanical strength, enhanced optical transparency, and a tunable work function, which can be modified through electrostatic or chemical doping [7,12–17]. The remarkable properties of graphene, including its exceptional electronic conductivity, thermal and mechanical strength, thermodynamic stability, robustness, and high carrier mobility, have sparked considerable interest for a wide range of applications [18–27]. Quantum dots (QDs) represent a unique class of materials that differ from both molecules and bulk substances. While retaining the structural and atomic consistency of bulk materials, QDs offer the distinct advantage of tuning their properties by adjusting a single parameter: the size of the particle [28–30]. Currently, achieving tunable and effective photoluminescence (PL) characterized by narrow emission spectra and photostability, along with the use of core-shell structures, is a common practice across various quantum dot (QD) material systems [31–33]. As a result, QDs have been seamlessly integrated as pivotal components in a diverse range of device applications. Numerous commercially available applications stemming from these advancements have become an integral part of our daily lives, exemplified by the prevalence of QD-based displays in various consumer products [34–36].

GQDs represent a new class of quantum dots, consisting of mono- or multi-layer graphene with exceptionally small dimensions, typically in the range of a few nanometers. They exhibit unique properties derived from the combined characteristics of graphene and quantum dots, resulting from quantum confinement and edge effects [37–41]. The significant focus on GQDs is due to their nanoscale size and the presence of sp2-sp2 carbon bonds in these minute graphene particles [42]. Due to their attractive features, including high electron mobility, adjustable band gaps, favorable biocompatibility, and effective solubility in various solvents, GQDs have been explored for the strategy and construction of innovative platforms across diverse areas such as fuel cells, light-emitting diodes, photovoltaics, drug delivery, biosensing and bioimaging [43–47]. Recently, attention has been directed towards the electrochemical capacitive features of GQDs, which play a key role in micro-supercapacitors. Studies involving carbon QDs/RuO2 and GQDs-carbon nanotubes (CNTs)-based catalysts indicate a significant enhancement in the performance of supercapacitors with the incorporation of GQDs [48]. GQDs can be prepared by two distinct routes: top-down and bottom-up methods. Top-down strategies involve the reduction of carbon fibers, macro-graphene sheets, CNTs or graphite into smaller components, essentially cutting them down [49–52]. On the side, bottom-up approaches utilize various small molecules as initial building blocks to construct the GQDs. This categorization highlights the diverse techniques employed in GQD synthesis, offering flexibility in the selection of starting materials and methodologies [53]. To broaden the usage of pure GQDs, various methods can be employed to functionalize them, customizing their properties for various applications. Functionalization, as illustrated in Fig. 1 , includes techniques such as heteroatoms doping, creating nanocomposites with inorganic or polymeric materials, and tuning the size and morphology of GQDs [54,55]. This modification can enhance optical, chemical, and electronic characteristics, enabling a diverse range of applications [56,57]. A crucial objective among these improvements is enhancing quantum yields (QY). In the early stages of GQDs synthesis, functionalization and assembly are in their nascent phases. The potential of GQD functionalization lies in its ability to modify PL, enhance biocompatibility, and alter electronic properties, thereby reducing non-radiative reabsorption and improving optical characteristics and QY. Notably, functionalization has demonstrated the production of GQDs with significant up-conversion and impressive QY [58,59]. Recently, GQDs featuring transition metal nanoparticles have adhered to their surfaces and have been utilized in the hydrolysis process, demonstrating effectual HER [60]. The introduction of heteroatoms has been observed to boost catalytic activity by influencing the change in local-spin or charge circulation by booming irregular charges at the interface of the nanomaterials.

Numerous studies have highlighted the use of heteroatom-doped GQDs with nitrogen (N)-doping being particularly prevalent. Nitrogen doping is widely employed to modify the intrinsic properties of GQDs, as nitrogen atoms have five valence electrons and an atomic radius similar to that of carbon, making them effective for tuning GQD characteristics [60–63]. Beyond N, boron (B)-atoms, having an identical atomic radius, are also sought after for their ease of bonding with carbon, resulting in minimal structural disorder [64]. Consequently, the B-doped GQDs has garnered considerable attention owing to its unique properties [65]. Furthermore, the construction step includes the use of potent oxidizing elements, leading to the inevitable inclusion of oxygen (O)-incorporated to GQDs. These functional groups predominantly include, epoxy (-O-), carboxyl (-COOH), carbonyl (-(CO)-), hydroxyl (-OH), and ether (-OCH3), groups [66]. The existence of O-functional groups significantly enhances the solubility of GQDs and also brings about alterations in their optical and electronic properties. Consequently, investigating the impact of these attached functional groups on the HER activity of GQDs becomes an intriguing avenue of exploration [67]. It is feasible to assess the impact of doping by examining alterations in the PL color, conductivity, and water solubility of GQDs. This section will provide an introduction to diverse heteroatom doping approaches and methods. The modifications in properties may be attributed to the electronegativity and fundamental characteristics of the dopant atoms. Furthermore, it is noteworthy that GQDs can undergo doping with single, double, and multiple heteroatoms [54]. Altering the electronic arrangement and surface chemical characteristics of GQDs through heteroatom doping, such as B, N, O, phosphorus (P), fluorine (F), and sulfur (S), proves to be an effective approach [68,69]. This process results in adjustable multicolor emission [70]. Additionally, it is observed that heteroatom doping enhances both the QD and potential performance of GQDs in detection of analytes [71,72]. The introduction of heteroatoms into the carbon lattice disrupts its uniformly conjugated electronic network. This modulation of the network affects the interface properties by altering charge circulation and the arrangement of doped domains.

In this review, we explore the production of heteroatom-doped GQDs and highlight their unique properties that make them highly beneficial for a wide range of biomedical applications, including biosensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, and therapeutic interventions. The ability to modify the properties of GQDs through heteroatom doping is a powerful strategy for enhancing their functionality and tailoring them for specific applications. By introducing various functional elements such as nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, and boron into the graphene lattice, it is possible to fine-tune the optical, electronic, and catalytic properties of GQDs. These modifications enable the development of GQDs with distinct features, such as phosphorescence emission, strong fluorescence, and enhanced catalytic activity, all of which are crucial for advancing their role in biomedical technologies. Particularly noteworthy is the ability of heteroatom-doped GQDs to exhibit multimodal imaging capabilities, where they serve as versatile platforms for both diagnostic and therapeutic applications. For instance, doping can enhance fluorescence for bioimaging applications while simultaneously improving the GQDs’ catalytic activity for targeted drug delivery or therapeutic intervention. These multifaceted properties open the door to integrated systems that combine imaging, biosensing, and therapy into a single platform, offering significant advantages for real-time monitoring and personalized medicine. Furthermore, the tunability of GQD properties through heteroatom doping can provide the necessary precision and functionality required for specific applications, including the development of novel biosensors, imaging agents, and therapeutic nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N- and S doped GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
N- and S doping of GQDs involves introducing nitrogen and sulfur atoms into the structure of GQDs, altering their electronic properties. This modification is crucial as it enhances the quantum dots' catalytic, optical, and electronic properties, making them promising candidates for various applications such as sensors, catalysis, sensors, and energy storage devices [64,73,74]. The introduction of these heteroatoms can improve charge carrier mobility, increase catalytic activity, and widen the range of optical absorption, thereby expanding the potential applications of GQDs in diverse fields [68,75]. For instance, Zheng et al., reported a straightforward and eco-conscious approach for crafting highly fluorescent N-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) through a single-step pyrolysis process, utilizing citric acid (CA) and glycine (Gly) as the carbon and N-precursors, respectively. These N-GQDs possess a QY of 28.10%. Notably, the resultant N-GQDs showcase robust blue-fluorescence, with the emission peak occurred at 450 nm and excitation at 355 nm [76]. Jian et al., developed an uncomplicated and low-cost method for synthesizing N-GQDs by subjecting GQDs to hydrothermal treatment in the presence of hydrazine, as presented in Fig. 2
A. The resultant N-GQDs, featuring abundant O-rich functionalities, demonstrate a pronounced blue-emission with a QY of 23.3% [77]. Yan and colleagues have introduced a straightforward electrochemical method for synthesizing N-GQDs with larger O-functional groups. In contrast to N-free GQDs, these developed N-GQDs exhibit blue luminescence that demonstrate electrocatalytic performance akin to a saleable Pt/C sample for ORR under alkaline conditions, showcasing a nitrogen-to-carbon atomic ratio of approximately 4.3% [78]. Gao and colleagues successfully produced N-GQDs through a hydrothermal synthesis approach utilizing p-phenylenediamine and p-coumaric acid, as shown in Fig. 2 B. These N-GQDs exhibit noteworthy characteristics, particularly their outstanding solubility and enhanced photostability. Additionally, the synthesized N-GQDs demonstrate uniformity in size distribution, with an average diameter of approximately 3.8±0.5nm [79]. Linbo and colleagues have outlined a method in their study (Fig. 2 C) where they employ a single-step thermolysis process for the preparation of N-GQDs with enhanced luminescent properties. This technique utilizes glycine as a carbon and nitrogen source. The resulting N-GQDs exhibit fluorescence that varies depending on the excitation wavelength, with peak excitation and emission wavelengths observed at 380 nm. Furthermore, they achieved a notable quantum yield of approximately 16% [80]. Qu and co-workers utilized CA as a source of carbon, which, when subjected to a basic environment, underwent self-assembly into sheet-like structures (Fig. 2 D). This process facilitated the formation of nitrogen-free GQDs via intermolecular dehydrolysis reactions. To introduce nitrogen functionalities, various nitrogen-containing bases, such as urea, were employed to prepare N-GQDs. An extensive examination of the structural and property characteristics provided insights into the mechanism behind the formation of N-GQDs, allowing for the manipulation of their optical emissions [81].

Hai and co-workers conducted a synthesis of S-doped GQDs (S-GQDs) using a high-temperature hydrothermal process (Fig. 3
A), wherein α-D-glucose and mercaptosuccinic acid (MSA) served as carbon and sulfur precursor resources, respectively. The process involved the fabrication of green-colored S-GQDs through hydrothermal treatment in the presence of D-glucose and MSA. The resulting S-GQDs exhibited a higher S-content of 3.7%, which was effectively doped onto the GQDs. This S-doping led to a significant enhancement in the QY, reaching 71% [82]. Sujata et al., introduced an innovative approach, marking the initial instance of a straightforward, cost-effective, and scalable synthesis method for producing sustainable, exceptionally crystalline S-GQDs through a hydrothermal method utilizing second-generation bio-waste, all achieved without the need for templates or catalysts. By analyzing X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD), they uncovered the intricate mechanism governing the transformation of precursor materials into S-GQDs. Notably, these S-GQDs, derived directly from bio-waste sources without surface passivation, attained the maximum QY recorded to date, reaching approximately 47% [83]. Sachin and co-workers conducted a study where they synthesized S-GQDs through a straightforward process involving the pyrolysis of CA to provide carbon and 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) to introduce sulfur as a doping agent. Through systematic experimentation, they identified the ideal reaction conditions, including the appropriate ratio of carbon to dopants, reaction temperature, and reaction duration, which significantly influenced the QY and fluorescence characteristics of the GQDs. These findings, representing a novel contribution to the field, highlight the potential of S-GQDs in various applications (Fig. 3 B). Additionally, the study demonstrated that the fluorescence property of the S-GQDs was notably diminished by the presence of quercetin, attributed to an inner-filter-effect (IFE) mechanism. The synthesis of the S-GQDs was accomplished using a previously reported method, with minor adjustments, employing a bottom-up process involving direct-pyrolysis of CA and MPA [84]. Yue et al., achieved the effective synthesis of a hybrid material called S-GQDs decorated carbon nanoparticles (S-GQDs/CNPs) through an in-situ process utilizing readily available fructose and sulfuric acid (H2SO4) under hydrothermal conditions (Fig. 3 C). Initially, the fructose molecules undergo dehydration facilitated by concentrated H2SO4, leading to the formation of a honeycomb-like carbon structure containing sulfate radicals during the subsequent hydrothermal treatment. Furthermore, the presence of H2SO4 not only catalyzes the reaction but also provides sulfur atoms essential for the incorporation of sulfur into the carbon material [85]. Shiyue and colleagues introduced an innovative approach for synthesizing S-GQDs through a one-pot method utilizing water-phase molecular fusion involving 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TTP), NaOH, and Na2S under hydrothermal conditions. This method yielded a 75% production rate, enabling the efficient production of S-GQDs characterized by higher crystalline nature. These S-GQDs emitted a constant yellow-green light across a broad pH ranging of 2.0 to 11.0 and demonstrated PL behavior that remained consistent regardless of excitation conditions [86].

Zhimin et al., reported a straightforward method that utilizes solvothermal processes enhanced by microwave irradiation to synthesize N- and S-co-doped reduced GO, which is denoted as N, S-RGO/GQDs (Fig. 4
A). The resulting hybrid nanomaterial, N, S-RGO/GQDs, serves as a non-metal electrocatalyst, exhibiting favorable catalytic characteristics for the ORR via a 4-electron pathway in alkaline conditions. Notably, these hybrids demonstrate enduring operational stability and resilience against methanol crossover effects, indicating promising prospects for practical applications in electrochemical systems [87]. The researchers modified GQDs by introducing both nitrogen and S-dopants, resulting in the creation of numerous localized energy levels close to the conduction band (Fig. 4 B). This modification led to a significant improvement in the fluorescence-quenching effect when compared to N or S-GQDs. The enhanced fluorescence quenching effect was observed specifically in a solution containing only 90 μM of 2, 4, 6-trinitrophenol (TNP), attributed to charge transfer interactions among the doped states. This selective enhancement enables the sensitive detection of TNP [88].

Tanvir and co-workers proposed a novel system to synthesize N-GQDs and NS-GQDs, which exhibit strong emission in both visible and NIR areas. The synthesis process involves a one-step microwave-supported hydrothermal method, utilizing a single starting material, glucosamine-HCl (with thiourea used as a precursor for NS-GQDs). The resulting N-GQDs and NS-GQDs are characterized by their unique-dispersed nature, with typical sizes of 5.50 and 3.90 nm, respectively. They also exhibit high crystallinity and a distinct G-band. These QDs were formed through amine linkages and various O-containing functional groups from the glucosamine, as confirmed by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Additionally, NS-GQDs contain approximately 2% sulfur [89]. Chaojie and colleagues established a groundbreaking approach to synthesizing GQDs that are dually doped with N and S. This pioneering method utilizes a straightforward and environmentally friendly pyrolysis process, incorporating CA as source of carbon and D-penicillamine as a dopant. The resulted N,S/GQDs exhibit exceptional properties, including excellent water solubility, minimal cytotoxicity, and a remarkably high QY of 56.4% [90]. Wissuta and co-workers synthesized N, S-GQDs using a hydrothermal synthesis approach, and demonstrating their efficacy as a sensitive and selective fluorescent tool for identifying cobalt ions (Co2+). These N, S-GQDs exhibit promising performance in detecting Co2+ in actual water samples, employing the conventional addition method with acceptable results. Additionally, they have devised N, S-GQD-infused paper strips, facilitating rapid and visually intuitive screening of Co2+. The synthesis of N, S-GQDs was accomplished through hydrothermal techniques, building upon prior methodologies as described in existing literature [91].

Fluorine doping of GQDs entails incorporating fluorine atoms into the structure, which modifies their electronic characteristics, boosting conductivity and stability. This doping is essential for enhancing performance in electronics, sensors, and catalysis by adjusting energy levels and facilitating charge transfer [47,92,93]. The synthesis of fluorinated GQDs (FGQDs) has proven to be a significant challenge, primarily because of the highly stable nature of the carbon-fluorine (C-F) bond, resulting in limited efforts being made towards the preparation of the above material. Wenbin et al., successfully synthesized FGQDs that involve the treatment of carbon fibers with fluorine by ultrasonic liquid-phase exfoliation. The described method is characterized by its simplicity, convenience, and affordability, thereby presenting a viable alternative for producing FGQDs. The resulting FGQDs display remarkable photoelectrical properties and impressive abilities to absorb ultraviolet light. The procedure for creating FGQDs is depicted in Fig. 5
A.

The elemental composition and chemical bonding of a substance are intricately tied to the manner in which it is prepared. To delve deeper into the composition and chemical bonds of the FGQDs [94]. Peiwei and colleagues utilized a new, gentle, and efficient approach to precisely craft FGQDs with adjustable fluorine content and dimensions, employing readily available fluorinated graphite. This technique circumvents the intricate processes associated with fluorinated graphene preparation and eliminates the use of hazardous gases, making it achievable under mild conditions. Furthermore, the resulting FGQDs demonstrate consistent sizing, exhibit excellent water solubility and stability, and showcase remarkably intense blue luminescence [97]. Antonova and co-workers conducted a synthesis of films comprising fluorinated graphene (FG)-integrated GQDs (FGGQDs) through chemical functionalization in a hydrofluoric acid solution. The investigation revealed significant fluctuations in the potential landscape within the FG barriers, with variations reaching as high as 200 mV [98]. Maryam et al., designed a protocol for the synthesis of fluorescent FGQDs via a one-step process, as shown in Fig. 5 B. These FGQDs, which possess a diameter of less than 3 nm, were produced via a microwave-assisted hydrothermal technique. Notably, the FGQDs exhibit both strong fluorescence and are capable of dispersing in water. In vitro experiments demonstrated the FGQDs' ability to hinder the aggregation and mitigate the cytotoxic effects of human islet amyloid polypeptide (hIAPP). Additionally, circular dichroism spectroscopy revealed that FGQDs could impede the structural transition of the peptide from its native conformation to β-sheet formation. Furthermore, FGQDs exhibited a dose-dependent capability to rescue INS-1 cell cytotoxicity induced by hIAPP [95]. Liu and colleagues developed a method to produce FGQDs with a high yield by employing a straightforward gaseous phase heating technique, wherein GQDs were combined with XeF2. The resulting FGQDs boasted a remarkable fluorine-to-carbon (F/C) atomic ratio of 84.25% and displayed a diameter ranging from 2 to 6 nanometers. Notably, the PL emission of the FGQDs demonstrated a pronounced blue-shift of 90 nm when compared to their original GQDs [99]. Changxing et al., designed a simple synthetic method for F and N-co-doped GQDs (F, N-GQDs) using levofloxacin and a straightforward hydrothermal approach, as depicted in Fig. 5 C. Their comprehensive investigation reveals that F, N-GQDs display a diverse range of fluorescence emissions spanning from blue to green when dispersed in different solvents. Furthermore, adjusting the concentrations of F, N-GQDs allows for the generation of multi-color fluorescence. Beyond these distinctive optical properties, F, N-GQDs demonstrate remarkable sensitivity to tetracycline, boasting an exceptionally low limit of detection (LOD) of 77 nM in an aqueous environment [96].

Amine doping of GQDs entails integrating amine functional groups into the graphene lattice, which boosts their solubility, stability, and compatibility with biological systems [100]. Amine doping enhances the charge carrier mobility of the quantum dots and promotes their interaction with biomolecules, rendering them versatile tools in biotechnology and nanomedicine [101]. Mayank and colleagues developed a protocol for the synthesis of a-GQDs through a facile and viable hydrothermal method (Fig. 6
A). These QDs exhibited a high QY of 51%, which is among the highest values reported for this type of nanostructured material. Leveraging the strong fluorescence properties of a-GQDs, the researchers linked them with anti-ferritin antibodies (Ab) for the purpose of fluorescence-based detection of ferritin. The synthesis process involved the utilization of powdered GO as a precursor, prepared using Hummer's method. Notably, this study represents the first documented instance of employing aminoterephthalic acid (ATA) as an amine source for the synthesis of a-GQDs [102]. Feng et al., conducted a study wherein they synthesized a-GQDs of adjustable sizes through an environmentally friendly approach, utilizing GO nanosheets, ammonia (NH3), and H2O2 as initial components. Employing a sephadex G-25 gel column for precise separation, they successfully isolated GQDs with either mono- or multilayers, marking a significant advancement. The resulting a-GQDs demonstrated notably low cytotoxicity and exceptional biocompatibility, in addition to showcasing multi-wavelength imaging capabilities. Moreover, the study revealed a novel finding: these particular GQDs exhibited promising antimycoplasma properties, representing a notable discovery in the field [103]. Tran et al., conducted a study where they synthesized a-GQDs through a single-step process involving microwave-assisted conversion of glucose, H2O2, and NH3 solution (Fig. 6 B). These QDs demonstrate superior structural integrity akin to graphene, showcasing with a diameter of 3.78 nm, along with a remarkable emission of intense green fluorescence with a high QY. Notably, these functionalized dots exhibit exceptional absorption capabilities under visible light [104]. Wang et al., reported a preparation process of dodecylamine-functionalized GQDs (D-GQDs) and their application as stabilizing agents in the emulsion polymerization process of styrene. Initially, D-GQDs were synthesized via thermal pyrolysis of CA with dodecyl amine in a solution containing 0.1 M NH4OH (Fig. 6 B). The resulting D-GQDs exhibited a structure comprising small graphene sheets adorned with an array of functional groups, including hydroxyl, acylamino, amino, carboxyl, and alkyl chains along the edges. This amphiphilic configuration renders the D-GQDs with notably higher surface activity. The introduction of D-GQDs into the system leads to a reduction in the surface tension of water to 30.8 mN m-1, and the interfacial tension of paraffin oil and water was calculated as 0.0182 mN m-1. Subsequently, these D-GQDs are harnessed as solid particle surfactants to stabilize styrene-in-water emulsions [105]. Zhenqian et al., introduced a new method for producing GQDs utilizing a one-pot hydrothermal process (Fig. 6 C). This technique involved the synthesis of GQDs containing bi-functional groups (-NH2 and -OH) by employing a poly-cyclic aromatic hydrocarbon as the precursor, specifically 1, 5-dinitronaphthalene, which underwent graphitization in an ammonia-water solution. The resulting GQDs possessed mono-layer structures with a size range of 1-3 nm, averaging 1.5 nm. The presence of alkaline species was found to be essential in facilitating the formation of a-GQDs.

Notably, the synthesized GQDs exhibited remarkable pH-sensitive PL performance, with fluorescence colors shifting from bluish-green to yellow as the pH varied from 5 to 10. This variation in fluorescence was ascribed to the protonation/de-protonation of the -NH2 groups in acidic or alkaline solution [106]. Tran et al., devised a straightforward and environmentally friendly method to synthesize a-GQDs with remarkable fluorescence properties. Through a single-step hydrothermal process, they created these a-GQDs from glucose as the source of carbon and NH3 as complexing mediator, avoiding the need for harsh chemicals like strong acids or oxidants. The resulting a-GQDs, characterized by a homogeneous size ranging from 3 to 4 nm (Fig. 6 D), boasted abundant amino groups and emitted a vivid green light with an impressive QY of 32.8%. This method yielded an aqueous solution containing a-GQDs at a concentration of approximately 1.7 mg/mL, achieving a yield of the product was about 53% [107]. Hanjun et al., described an approach involving the development of a novel type of fluorescent probe designed for detecting Cu2+ ions. This breakthrough involves subjecting GQDs to a hydrothermal treatment process, resulting in the transformation of greenish-yellow fluorescent GQDs (referred to as g-GQDs) with a relatively low QY of 2.5% into a-GQDs exhibiting a significantly enhanced QY of 16.4%. Notably, this conversion occurs through hydrothermal treatment of NH3. Additionally, the resulting a-GQDs were characterized as biocompatible and environmentally friendly. Of particular interest is the observation that the positively charged nature of the a-GQDs facilitates their uptake by cells, thereby enabling the detection of Cu2+ ions within living cellular environments. This development holds promise for applications in bioimaging and biomedical research [108]. Blanco and colleagues have successfully developed a method for creating a-GQDs with luminescent up conversion capabilities. This innovative approach utilizes a straightforward and environmentally friendly process that incorporates the application of powerful ultrasonic waves and high-intensity microwaves under hydrothermal conditions. Notably, this technique eliminates the need for harsh chemical agents such as strong acids or oxidizing agents. By subjecting the graphite precursor to mechanical treatment via ultrasonic waves and subsequently processing the resulting materials hydrothermally in a pressurized microwave oven with concentrated NH3, the researchers achieve the synthesis of a-GQDs with precise size and desirable electronic properties [109]. Shuxuan et al., introduced a groundbreaking category of thin-film nanocomposites (TFN) for spiral-wound reverse osmosis nanofiltration (SRNF) membranes. These membranes were meticulously crafted using a sequence of processes, including chemical imidization, solvent activation, interfacial polymerization (IP), and crosslinking. Through this method, they were able to establish covalent bonds not only between the IP-layer and the substrate, but also between the IP-layer and surrounded a-GQDs. The obtained a-GQDs exhibited an average nanosheet size of approximately 7.2 nm and a typical thickness of 1.6 nm, which involved a vital role in improving the performance of the membranes. This innovative approach represents a significant advancement in membrane technology for water purification and other applications [110]. Tetsuka and co-workers documented their discovery of optically modifiable a-GQDs, distinguished by discrete molecular weights and distinct edges, which were derived via the NH3-mediated bond-cleavage reaction from GOx sheets. These a-GQDs display a striking array of multiple colors when subjected to single-wavelength UV excitation, boasting luminance QY exceeding 40%. The nuanced optical characteristics observed are intricately governed solely by the quantitative functionalization of the a-GQDs, a conclusion corroborated by thorough MALDI-TOF mass analysis and density functional theory calculations [111].

Tran and colleagues developed boron-doped GQDs (BGQDs) using a straightforward, efficient, and mild temperature approach involving the hydrothermal treatment of glucose with boric acid as starting materials (Fig. 7
A). The resulting BGQDs exhibit a notable boron doping level of up to 4.25% and display consistent nanometer-scale dimensions. Additionally, they synthesized a composite material consisting of rGO and BGQDs (referred to as G-BGQD) to serve as a metal-free catalyst toward the ORR (Fig. 7 B) [112].

Shuyan et al., synthesized BGQDs through a facile hydrothermal process, as shown in Fig. 7 C. This involved a one-step bottom-up molecular fusion between TTP, and borax in NaOH. The synthesis was carried out in a large-volume autoclave and achieved a high yield of 71%, indicating the feasibility of producing BGQDs on a gram-scale. The resulting BGQDs displayed characteristics such as a mono or bilayered graphene structure, higher crystallinity, unique size distribution, and intense green fluorescence with an absolute PL with a QY of 16.8%. Notably, their fluorescence was independent of excitation, with maximum excitation and emission observed at 480 and 520 nm, respectively. Given its high yield and exceptional properties, this synthesis approach holds ability for large-scale synthesis of BGQDs [113].

Glycine doping of GQDs involves incorporating the amino acid glycine into the structure of GQDs during their synthesis. Glycine doping can also improve the quantum dots' stability and biocompatibility, opening up possibilities for advanced biomedical applications such as drug delivery and cancer therapy [114]. For instance, Rui and co-workers developed a straightforward and environmentally friendly pyrolysis technique using ethylene glycol as a carbon source and glycine as a functional compound to synthesize GLY-GQDs (Fig. 8 ).

These GLY-GQDs demonstrated outstanding water solubility and possessed a remarkable fluorescence QY of 21.7%. Furthermore, the synthesized GLY-GQDs exhibited enduring stability against both light exposure and extended periods of storage, making them a promising material for various applications [115]. Qianqian et al., reported an efficient approach to produce GLY-GQDs by modifying GQDs with GLY under alkaline environments. The resulting GLY-GQDs demonstrate outstanding blue-emission at 444 nm, which remains consistent regardless of the excitation source, and boast a notably QY of 35.7%. These GLY-GQDs exhibit a typical size of 5.9 nm and a fine size distribution [116].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of hydroxyl doped (OH-GQDs) and valine doped GQDs (V-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para:
Hydroxyl and valine doping of GQDs enhances their properties, including stability and functionality. Hydroxyl groups can improve dispersibility and biocompatibility, crucial for biomedical applications [117,118]. Valine doping enhances conductivity and can tailor surface chemistry, vital for sensors and energy devices. These doping strategies expand the versatility and performance of GQDs, unlocking their potential in various fields from medicine to electronics [119]. For instance, Sarun et al., reported a novel gas sensor for room-temperature applications by employing a method involving the deposition of OH-GQDs onto a nickel electrode with interdigitated features. These OH-GQDs were prepared through a bottom-up method utilizing the hydrothermal process of pyrene (Fig. 9
A). The functionalization of OH-GQDs was thoroughly examined through a combination of gas-sensing analysis and self-consistent charge density functional tight-binding (SCC-DFTB) calculation. The outcomes revealed that this gas sensor, operating at room temperature, exhibited notable sensitivity and selectivity, along with rapid response rates when exposed to NH3 within a concentration range spanning from 10 to 500 ppm, displaying a linear correlation [120]. Zhou et al., conducted a synthesis method for valine-GQDs (val-GQDs) through the thermal decomposition of CA and valine (Fig. 9 B). The resultant val-GQDs possess an average diameter of 3 nm, with the edges of the graphene nanosheets featuring an abundance of hydrophilic groups, thereby enhancing their solubility in water. In comparison to GQDs synthesized solely from CA via thermal decomposition, val-GQDs demonstrate significantly heightened fluorescence, exhibiting an approximate tenfold increase along with superior photostability with a fourfold enhancement [121]

After achieving successful synthesis of functionalized GQDs, it becomes advantageous to pinpoint the most suitable and commonly employed techniques for characterizing and studying the properties of functionalized GQDs, as each method offers insights into distinct aspects of their exclusive optical, and physical-chemical features. Given that functionalized GQDs exhibit distinct properties, it is imperative to employ various characterization techniques to measure and establish correlations among their optical, physical, and chemical attributes [122]. The examination of GQDs can be divided into many aspects, such as:

When it comes to morphologically properties of functionalized GQDs, two highly notable techniques are often employed by TEM and AFM techniques [123,124]. These microscopic methods have gained widespread usage in materials and chemistry laboratories, allowing for precise size measurements of functionalized GQDs by TEM and surface topography by AFM, which provides information about the number of graphitic layers [125]. Moreover, TEM proves valuable not only for determining size but also for evaluating the crystallinity of formed GQDs. Among the two techniques, TEM is considered more accessible due to its precise analysis and technological advances [126]. Other similar techniques including high resolution TEM (HRTEM) and SEM were also used to characterize the morphological features of GQDs. Wang and colleagues conducted TEM analysis operating at 200 keV to examine the as-prepared D-GQDs, which showed a nearly two-dimensional nanosheet morphology with minimal agglomeration (Fig. 10
A). Utilizing image processing software, a particle size distribution histogram was generated, and revealing the size of graphene nanosheets within the D-GQDs falls within a size range of 3-9 nm, and an average nanoparticle size of 5.6 nm (Fig. 10 B). Furthermore, topographical imaging was executed using a Multimode-8 AFM (Bruker, Germany). The AFM image of D-GQDs depicted in Fig. 10 C-D shows that their height profiles measure less than 2.0 nm, implying the presence of one to five layers of GO like structures [105]

To prepare samples for HRTEM analysis, one common method involves dispersing the desired material, like GQDs, through sonication in a suitable solvent blend such as ethanol, dimethylformamide (DMF) or water, which allows the formation of highly dispersed suspension. A small amount of this suspension (3-6 µL) is then drop casted onto perforated carbon-coated copper grids. After the solvent evaporates, the resulting dried grids are ready for HRTEM analysis. The HRTEM allows for the visualization and analysis of GQDs, which providing potential insights into their particle size, crystallinity, morphology, and other structural characteristics with high precision and detail [127]. Zhimin et al., conducted an investigation utilizing HRTEM to examine the structural characteristics of N, S-RGO/GQDs hybrids and N, S-GQDs. Their experiments were performed on a JEOL JEM-2011F electron microscope operating at 200 kV. The analyzed N, S-RGO/GQDs nanohybrids displayed a consistent distribution of N, S-GQDs with diameters ranging from 1.5 to 4.0 nm, dispersed over a layer of rGO containing N and S-dopants (N, S-RGO). These GQDs exhibited similarities to GQDs or N-doped GQDs synthesized via electrochemical methods, as depicted in Fig. 11
A and B. High-resolution TEM images (Fig. 11 C and D) and corresponding SAED depicted inset of Fig. 11 E) provided insight into the inter-layer spacing of the N, S-GQDs, which measured at 0.206 nm. Remarkably, this distance mirrors the interplanar spacing characteristic of diamond (d111 = 0.206 nm), as cited in prior literature [87]

Milenkovi and co-workers employed the synthesis of L-cystine incorporated GQDs, denoted as GQDs-cys-25, and GQDs-cys-200 samples, by mixing pristine salt in ethanolic solution, followed by drop-casting onto clean silicon substrates measuring 10mm × 10mm. Subsequently, the prepared solutions were dried under vacuum for 12 h. For SEM imaging, only the GQDs-cys-50 sample was deposited onto Al-support using the aforementioned procedure. Notably, the samples were analyzed by SEM analysis without any additional treatments, such as metallization, and in "low-vacuum mode". The EDX measurements were conducted by focusing the analysis over an area approximately 230 microns × 200 microns [128]. The surface characteristics and chemical configuration of irradiated GQD-cys were examined through SEM-EDS measurements, as depicted in Fig. 12 . The SEM images (Fig. 12 A, C, E, and G) were revealed that all samples exhibited similar surface morphology, typically a plate-like structures and clusters. The EDS investigation unveiled the elemental composition, showing that pristine GQDs contained only C and O-elements, whereas GQD-cys were comprised of C, O, and S-elements by post-irradiation process. Notably, C and O remained in the samples after irradiation. Silicon was also detected in all EDX spectra due to the substrate material (Fig. 12 B, D, F, and H). The atomic percentage of C and O was comparable for GQD-cys-25 and GQD-cys-50, with a decline observed in GQD-cys-200, indicating a reduction in O-containing functional groups. Additionally, S-doping was evident in all samples, with the highest level detected at 200 kGy irradiation mode, confirming successful incorporation of S into GQDs [128].

The characteristics of GQDs subjected to both p-and γ-irradiation were assessed using an AFM microscope, as depicted in Fig. 13
a-d. The AFM images and histograms provided insights into the height profile, morphology, and size distribution of these GQDs. It was observed that the height of the GQDs ranged from 0.5 to 2.5 nm, indicating a thickness equivalent to a single graphene layer, typically measuring between 0.7 and 1 nm. Notably, the analysis revealed that p-GQDs exhibited a lower proportion of single and double-layered GQDs, constituting only 5.26 and 6.31%, respectively. Consequently, the diameter of p-GQDs, determined through tip deconvolution, was measured at about 20.4 nm. Furthermore, variations in diameter were observed among different GQD-cys derivatives, with respective diameters of 25.5, 28.0, and 29.0 nm for GQD-cys-25, GQD-cys-50, and GQD-cys-200, as reported [128].

The examination of the crystalline properties of GQDs involves utilizing techniques such as XRD and Raman spectroscopy. XRD is a crucial method used for identifying the crystalline phases of nanomaterials, whether they are crystalline or amorphous. It delivers new insights into the degree of crystallinity and the dispersal of particle sizes in nanomaterials. Additionally, XRD helps to determine the precise atomic arrangements within the unit cell of a crystal by analyzing the diffraction patterns produced when X-rays interact with the nanocrystal's surface. This process involves plotting the intensity of the diffracted X-rays against the angle of detection, revealing valuable information about the nanocrystal's structure and composition [127]. Sachin and co-workers conducted XRD analysis utilizing an X-ray diffractometer, specifically the Bruker D8-Advance model, operating at an acceleration potential of 40 kV and employing a Cu Kα radiation source. The freshly prepared S-GQDs, exhibits a wide peak positioned approximately at 24°, specifically related to the (002) plane. This observation aligns well with previously documented findings in the scientific literature [84]. Zhang et al., performed XRD patterns for B-graphene, boron-free GQDs (B-free GQDs), and BGQDs, which exhibits a discernible shift towards a higher degree, approximately at 25.8°, which is comparable to pristine graphene obtained under similar conditions, which typically manifests at around 24.3°. This shift indicates a reduction in interlayer spacing due to the more thorough reduction facilitated by the presence of B2O3. Similarly, B-free GQDs exhibit a wider diffraction peak at approximately 25°, suggesting an oxidation reaction during the synthesis process, which introduces larger active sites on BGQDs [65]

Raman spectroscopy serves as a valuable complementary technique in the characterization of GQDs. This analytical method proves particularly useful in identifying and quantifying the presence of G- and D bands hybridized carbon within GQD structure [124]. In essence, the detection of sp2 carbon signifies a graphitic structure, whereas sp3 carbons are indicative of defect sites and aliphatic carbons situated on the periphery of the GQDs [129]. The relative intensities of both G and D bands, often expressed as a ratio, involve a critical role in reporting the extent of defects present in the synthesized GQDs. Moreover, this ratio facilitates the analysis of the degree of chemical modification undergone by GQDs under varying experimental conditions [130]. Sarun and co-workers conducted an analysis of OH-functionalized GQDs (OH-GQDs) using Raman spectroscopy (RS). Specifically, they utilized a Renishaw-model Invia instrument with an excitation wavelength of 785 nm. The resulting RS of OH-GQDs spans the spectral range of 1100-1800 cm-1. Notably, the spectrum exhibits distinct features indicative of graphitic structures, including the presence of D and G bands located approximately at 1350 and 1580 cm-1 respectively [120]. Tran Van Tam and co-workers conducted Raman spectroscopy analysis on both pristine and a-GQDs within the frequency range of 1000 to 2000 cm-1 at ambient temperature. This analysis was carried out using a RS equipped with a 532 nm excitation light source. The Raman spectra of pristine GQDs are depicted by the black line, while the spectra of a-GQDs are represented by the red line [104].

FT-IR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were generally employed to characterize the various components of GQDs. A diverse array of methodologies is used to analyze the chemical attributes of GQDs, encompassing techniques traditionally associated with both organic chemistry and materials chemistry analyses. The preeminent method for chemically characterizing GQDs is XPS, a technique that furnishes intricate insights into the elemental composition and bonding characteristics of the constituent atoms within GQDs [131]. XPS proves instrumental in verifying the successful integration of heteroatoms, elucidating precise bond structures between GQD atoms, and deducing the presence of definite functional groups [132]. For instance, Tran et al., conducted XPS analysis utilizing monochromatic Al-Ka radiation (with an energy source of 1486.6 eV) to examine the elemental composition of a-GQDs. The sample for XPS analysis was prepared by depositing 1 mL of a-GQDs solution (0.5 mg/mL) onto a SiO2/Si substrate, followed by natural dried at 25°C. The XPS survey spectrum of a-GQDs revealed distinct signals corresponding to oxygen (O 1s at approximately 532 eV), carbon (C 1s at around 285 eV), and a prominent nitrogen peak (N 1s at approximately 400 eV). Furthermore, the high-resolution C 1s XPS of a-GQDs exhibited peaks indicative of different carbon bonds, including C-C at 284.4 eV, C-N at 285.7 eV, carbon hydroxyl (C-OH) at 286.6 eV, and carboxylate carbon functionalities (O=C-O) at 288.7 eV. The existence of C-N suggested the presence of N-doping in QDs. Further analysis of the N 1s XPS revealed two distinct peaks at 399.3 and 401.2 eV, corresponding to NH2 and protonated-NH2 groups of a-GQDs, respectively. Hence, the XPS analysis indicate that NH2 groups likely form during synthesis and they contribute to the improving an optical activities of a-GQDs [104]

In the realm of GQDs, the second most prevalent method for chemical analysis involves the utilization of infrared (IR) spectroscopy. This technique proves invaluable for scrutinizing the array of chemical functional groups within nanomaterials [133]. However, it is noteworthy that the determination of the specific abundance of certain functional groups via IR is not always straightforward. For instance, hydroxyls and carbonyls tend to exhibit pronounced signals in an IR spectrum, overshadowing the relatively weaker signals from other functional groups. Despite this limitation, IR spectroscopy remains a valuable tool for assessing the success of reactions in a synthetic route. This is particularly relevant in cases where a new functional group is inserted or eliminated at various stages of the synthetic pathway. Moreover, it is common practice to combine IR spectroscopy with XPS to comprehensively study the elemental composition of GQDs [134]. Zhou and colleagues conducted an analysis using IR on val-GQDs, employing a Nicolet FT-IR 6700 spectrometer to examine the functional groups in the nanostructured material. Notably, the wide absorption peak observed at 3430 cm−1 signifies the stretching vibration characteristic of O-H bonds. Furthermore, a notable band around 1700 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of C=O bonds. Additionally, peaks positioned at 2970 and 1080 cm−1 are indicative of the stretching vibrations associated with C-H and C-O groups, respectively. Moreover, the presence of N-related bonds is evidenced by peaks observed at 3250, 2360, and 1210 cm−1. This comprehensive analysis reveals that the val-GQDs synthesized were primarily characterized by the presence of amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups [121]

FT-IR serves as a crucial technique for qualitatively identifying surface components and functional groups present on the materials. In this method, infrared radiation is directed through a sample, typically in the form of a small pellet, wherein some of the radiation is absorbed due to the molecular vibrations of the sample, while the rest transmits through. Through this process, specific signals are generated in what's known as the fingerprint region, each corresponding to the molecular vibrations of different molecules present. Researchers exploit this process to analyze the surface functional groups from GQDs, which play an essential role in tailoring their energy states and consequently influencing their optical properties [135]. Generally, the FT-IR of GQDs were observed around 3400, 1600, and 1350 cm−1, indicative of the O-H stretching vibrations, C=C aromatic stretching, and C=O from carboxylic groups, respectively, particularly for GQDs functionalized with carboxylic or hydroxyl groups. Though, it is significant to note that these functional groups can vary dependent on the intended application and the specific synthesis methods and precursors utilized [127]. Tanvir et al. conducted FT-IR analysis on N-GQDs/NS-GQDs using attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode. The sample utilized for the analysis was freeze-dried and dehydrated. The resulting spectra displayed prominent bands at 3275 and 3090 cm−1 (as depicted in Fig. 14
A and B), which are associated with O-H stretching vibrations and N-H groups, respectively, with a distinct emphasis on the presence of O-H. Furthermore, identifiable peaks were observed at 2935, 1602, 1530, and 1412 cm−1, corresponding to vibrational modes of C-H, C-O (indicative of COOH), C-C, and C-O-C. Moreover, stretching vibrations of C-OH, C-N/N-H/C-H, and C-O were also detected at 1330, 1240, and 1021 cm−1, respectively [89]

The robust luminescence exhibited by GQDs has led to the widespread utilization of UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy for evaluating the photophysical attributes of these materials. UV-vis spectroscopy enables the determination of the specific wavelength at which GQDs absorb light, promoting the transition of valence electrons to excited states [136]. Subsequently, this absorbed wavelength serves as the excitation wavelength in fluorescence spectroscopy, facilitating the measurement of the material's fluorescence emission. Fluorescence spectroscopy is instrumental in deducing characteristics such as fluorescence life-time and QY [137,138]. This optical spectroscopic technique forms the basis of numerous sensing platforms employing GQDs, wherein alterations in fluorescence emission-whether enhancement or quenching-can be quantified upon the interaction of GQDs with a choice of analyte [139,140]. Although it is not a direct characterization method, fluorescence microscopy is usually employed to illustrate GQDs sensing substrates, especially in scenarios where GQDs are integrated into biological systems [141]. Sachin and co-workers conducted experiments to analyze the optical properties, including fluorescence spectra, of as-synthesized SGQDs. They utilized an F-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer to measure wavelengths from 300 to 600 nm and a Cary 5000 UV–Vis-NIR spectrometer for wavelengths ranging from 200 to 800 nm. The characteristic absorption peak observed at 360 nm, as depicted in Fig. 15
A, is attributed to n-π* electronic transitions, likely due to the presence of C-OH or C=O groups within the SGQD structure [142]. This absorption band at 360 nm is suggested to corresponding to the optimal PL excitation peak of SGQDs (Fig. 15 B). Notably, the aqueous solution of SGQDs, exhibiting a pale-yellow color, emitted blue fluorescence when introduced to UV-radiation with a wavelength of 365 nm, indicating remarkable luminescent and dispersion properties [142]. Additionally, the PL emission spectra of SGQDs at various excitation wavelength spanning from 300 to 400 nm, revealing emission features independent of excitation wavelength [84]. Rui et al., conducted experiments where they analyzed the UV-visible absorption and PL spectra of GLY-GQDs under varying excitation wavelength, as depicted in Fig. 15 C, and D. Their findings reveal that GLY-GQDs exhibit two distinct absorption bands: one peaking at 201 nm, likely stemming from the generation of various poly-aromatic chromophores, and another positioned at 328 nm attributed to the n-π* shift within GLY-GQDs. The photo-luminescent spectral result suggesting that the GLY-GQDs emission wavelength remains relatively consistent across various excitation wavelength [115]

FGQDs and their derivatives exhibit a broad array of remarkable properties, making them highly promising materials for a wide range of biomedical applications. Due to their unique optical, electronic, and biocompatible characteristics, FGQDs have shown significant potential in areas such as medicine, diagnostics, and drug delivery, with the ability to enhance both therapeutic outcomes and quality of life [131,143,144]. Their versatility has attracted considerable interest from both academia and industry, driving a growing body of research dedicated to exploring their biomedical applications. To optimize the performance of FGQDs, researchers have employed various strategies, including doping, size and shape control, and the development of advanced composite materials. These approaches aim to fine-tune the properties of FGQDs to meet the specific requirements of biomedical applications, enhancing their effectiveness as imaging agents, drug carriers, and biosensors. Looking ahead, it is expected that FGQDs will play an increasingly pivotal role in the development of next-generation biomedical technologies. Current applications that already demonstrate their potential include biological imaging, where FGQDs serve as highly efficient fluorescent probes for cellular and molecular imaging, as well as targeted drug delivery, enabling precise treatment with minimal side effects [145]. In addition, their role in diagnostics, photodetectors, and even therapeutic applications such as photothermal therapy is gaining momentum. These ongoing advancements highlight the vast and expanding potential of FGQDs to drive innovation in biomedicine, positioning them as a material of choice for a wide range of clinical and research applications [119,145–147].

The study by Dong et al. investigated the application of GQDs in bio-imaging, particularly focusing on their potential for cellular imaging of the MCF-7 human breast cancer cell-line. Their results demonstrated that GQDs exhibit remarkable fluorescence properties and low cytotoxicity, making them suitable for labeling various cellular components such as the cell membrane, cytoplasm, and nucleus. Notably, this research is significant as it signifies the first instance of utilizing fluorescent carbon materials as markers, specifically targeting the cell nuclei, thus paving the way for innovative approaches in cellular imaging and diagnosis [148]. Functionalized GQDs exhibit promising prospects in the realm of bioimaging due to their exceptional fluorescence characteristics and minimal cytotoxicity. In their study, Mila et al. conducted an analysis on the cellular uptake of various quantum dots, namely p-GQDs, GQD-cys-25, GQD-cys-50, and GQDs-cys-200, into HeLa cells using fluorescent microscopy. The results revealed that all tested QDs successfully entrapped by the HeLa cells. Notably, the treatment with GQDs-cys-25 yielded the most distinct visibility of HeLa cells [128]. Furthermore, upon calculating the PL QY, it was observed that GQD-cys-25 exhibited the highest values, despite the similar sizes of all samples ranging from 14 to 30 nm. The study also noted that quantum dots with sizes <100 nm entered the cell via a passive mechanism [149,150]. Though, the discrepancy in cell visibility was attributed to the superior PL of GQDs-cys-25, positioning it as the most promising candidate for bioimaging applications.

Zhao et al., conducted an investigation to evaluate the potential cellular toxicity associated with functionalized diamines, specifically focusing on phosphorus-GQDs (P-GQDs) and ethylene diamine GQDs (EDA-GQDs), through in vitro cytotoxicity assays using human Hela cells. The obtained results demonstrated that both P-GQDs and EDA-GQDs exhibited minimal toxic towards human Hela cells, with cell feasibilities exceeding 80% at concentrations below 125 mg mL-1. This level of toxicity was found to be comparable to that reported for CDs in earlier studies. Notably, EDA-GQDs demonstrated relatively lower toxicity compared to P-GQDs, suggesting that the introduction of organic molecules had minimal impact on the cellular toxicity of GQDs. Furthermore, the ability of both types of GQDs to label human Hela cells was compared. The findings revealed that both P-GQDs and EDA-GQDs were proficient in penetrating the cytoplasm and effectively labeling it. Additionally, due to the higher emission efficiency of EDA-GQDs relative to P-GQDs, smaller quantities of EDA-GQDs could be employed in cell-imaging applications to attain comparable fluorescence study [58]. Ding et al. introduced a novel theranostic nanoagent utilizing GQDs, capitalizing on their water solubility, biocompatibility, and non-toxic nature. This nanoagent was loaded with doxorubicin (DOX), enabling both therapeutic and diagnostic functions. Tracking the internalization process was facilitated via blue fluorescence that emitted by GQDs. Remarkably, the fluorescence of DOX was considerably quenched by the proximity of the GQDs. However, upon internalization, a distinct bright green fluorescence from DOX indicated its effective release from the nanoagent, leading to substantial therapeutic efficacy in terms of chemotherapeutic killing and notable inhibition of tumor growth. Later, Wang et al. introduced a novel class of fluorescent materials, namely orange fluorescent phosphorus-GQDs (PGQDs), and blue fluorescent BGQDs, tailored for in-vitro imaging purposes. In contrast, Campbell et al., presented a comprehensive study on the development of multicolor emissive GQDs, including S-, N-, and B-N co-doped GQDs, specifically designed for both visible and near-infrared (NIR-I) in-vitro imaging applications (Fig. 16 ) [151]

Zhu and colleagues demonstrated the potential of GQDs for bioimaging by incubating human osteosarcoma cells with a GQD suspension [152]. Using a confocal fluorescence microscope, they successfully observed the GQDs as they entered the cells through the cell membrane, confirming their suitability for cellular imaging. However, cationic GQDs tend to aggregate in aqueous solutions, which can limit their effectiveness. To address this issue, biocompatible polymer modifications are often applied to GQDs, improving their stability, dispersion, and overall functionality for biological applications [153]. Wen et al. developed fluorescent GQDs modified with organosilane groups, referred to as Si-GQDs, which they incorporated into mesoporous hollow silica nanospheres [154]. In experiments observing cellular uptake in HePG2 cells, these hybrid nanospheres emitted blue and green fluorescence when excited at 405 nm and 488 nm wavelengths, respectively. The findings suggest that these functionalized GQDs could be valuable tools for bioimaging, particularly for tracking the delivery of drugs or genes within biological systems [153]. Bioactive graphene quantum dot (GQD)-based polymer composites are emerging as highly promising materials for imaging applications in deeper tissue layers. These composites possess the advantageous property of emitting near-infrared (NIR) reflection, which is particularly useful in medical imaging [155]. This characteristic plays a crucial role in detecting metastasis, cancer recurrence, and diagnosing tumors at early stages through sensitive imaging techniques. Given the critical importance of imaging in cancer diagnosis, it is increasingly valued for its contribution to early detection and management. Fig. 17 provides a visual representation of cancer diagnosis using bioimaging methods based on bioactive GQDs. Like other nanoparticle systems, GQDs possess intrinsic photoluminescent (PL) properties, enabling them to function as optical probes in fluorescence imaging without the need for additional fluorescent dyes [156]. Recently, bioactive GQD-based polymer composites have demonstrated enhanced capabilities in both magnetic resonance (MR) imaging and near-infrared fluorescence imaging, broadening their potential applications in bioimaging. The distinct advantages of these composites include their adaptability as ideal imaging probes, supporting a variety of bioimaging techniques. Moreover, their excellent biocompatibility makes them highly suitable for clinical applications, offering the potential for significant advancements in bioimaging [157]. In the study conducted by Sheng et al., nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using citric acid, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP K90), and glutamate as precursors. The synthesized N-GQDs demonstrated effectiveness as a fluorescent probe for detecting chromium (VI) ions and showed potential as a marker for labeling MCF-7 cells, a commonly studied human breast cancer cell line [158]

Research has indicated the utilization of graphene or graphene-derived nanomaterials as effective carriers for drug delivery, aimed at enhancing delivery efficacy and therapeutic outcomes. In comparison to graphene, GQDs and FGQDs exhibit superior characteristics such as improved water solubility, reduced cytotoxicity, and increased specific surface area. Additionally, they possess the capability to form interactions with a diverse range of compounds through intermolecular π-π interactions. Consequently, GQDs and FGQDs emerge as more efficient carriers or vehicles for drug molecules, offering promising potential in pharmaceutical applications [159–161]. Vatanparast and co-workers conducted a thorough investigation into the interaction dynamics between 5-fluorouracil (FU) and both undoped and doped GQDs using DFT calculations [162]. Their findings unveiled promising potential for GQDs doped with aluminum nitride (AlN) and aluminum phosphide (AlP) to function as effective carriers for FU drugs within the realm of nanomedicine. Subsequently, they further utilized DFT-calculations to explore the viability of GQDs and doped GQDs as carriers for isoniazid (Iso). The obtained results provided compelling evidence supporting the utility of AlN- and AlP-GQDs as potential vehicles for drug delivery [163]. Moreover, in a more recent study, they delved into investigating the impact of various N-groups on the drug delivery efficacy of N-GQDs through a combination of DFT-calculations and MD-simulations. Notably, their analysis indicated that central N-GQDs exhibited greater drug release efficiency when compared to pristine GQDs and edge N-GQDs [164]. In their study, Ghanbari et al., utilized a hydrothermal approach to create N-GQDs emitting blue fluorescence. This synthesis method involved subjecting CA and urea to pyrolysis, serving as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. One noteworthy aspect of their work was the innovative incorporation of the anti-cancer drug methotrexate (MTX) onto the N-GQDs, resulting in MTX-(N-GQDs) as a promising drug delivery system. Particularly significant was their investigation into the impact of MTX-(N-GQDs) on human breast cancer cells in-vitro, revealing that the drug-free N-GQDs nanocarriers displayed excellent biocompatibility. In contrast, the MTX-loaded nanocarriers exhibited heightened cytotoxicity compared to free MTX alone. This highlights the successful application of GQDs as nanocarriers, effectively extending the cytotoxic effects of the loaded drug, thereby bolstering the elimination of cancer cells [165] Kumawat and co-workers introduced a novel method for producing self-assembled sGQDs utilizing grape seed extract (GSE) in a green synthesis approach. The choice of GSE is strategic due to its availability as a commercial product, ensuring consistency in composition compared to natural sources which may vary with seasonal and geographical factors. GSE, a rich source of polyphenols commonly found in dietary supplements, serves as a therapeutic carbon source for synthesizing sGQDs. Additionally, GSE exhibits various beneficial properties such as potent antioxidant, antimicrobial, anti-ulcer, and anti-cancer activities. Their research demonstrates that sGQDs synthesized with GSE facilitate rapid wound closure in vitro, as evidenced by an in-vitro wound scratch assay. Moreover, these sGQDs are shown to internalize into the nucleus, promoting accelerated cell proliferation, as depicted in Fig. 18 . Furthermore, the sGQDs exhibit a pH-responsive behavior, displaying a linear fluorescence profile to changes in a narrow pH range (pH 3–10). This unique property suggests the potential for developing cost-effective and scalable sensing probes in biomedical nanotechnology, offering promising applications in the field [166].

Qi and co-workers conducted a study where they enhanced GQDs by attaching specific ligands for nucleus targeting. They initially prepared a-GQDs and then modified them with TAT peptides, known for their cell-penetrating and nucleus-targeting properties, forming TAT-NGs. These TAT-NGs were further modified with FA-PEG, which helps in targeting cancer cells by binding to folate receptors and extending their circulation time in the bloodstream [167]. This modification process facilitated precise targeting of cancer cells and enhanced the therapeutic effects of the GQDs, as demonstrated in experiments on HeLa tumors both in-vitro and in-vivo. Additionally, their research described that FAPEG-TNGs could readily bind to DNA, supporting their observations of enhanced anticancer effects [168].

Moreover, intracellular investigations showed that FAPEG-TNGs induced DNA damage, leading to the activation of apoptosis-accelerating proteins and subsequent cell death (Fig. 19 ). Overall, their study showcases the rational design and development of GQDs targeted towards cancer cell nuclei, highlighting their effectiveness in selectively killing cancer cells through DNA damage [169]. Zhu and colleagues developed a multifunctional core–shell structured capsule designed to enhance controlled drug release and delivery for water-insoluble drugs. This capsule was composed of an olive oil core surrounded by a dual-layer porous shell made from titanium dioxide (TiO₂), with additional components of iron oxide (Fe₃O₄) and GQDs integrated into the structure [170]. The olive oil served as a reservoir for oil-soluble drugs, such as paclitaxel, allowing them to be stored within the capsule's core. The TiO₂ shell was essential in preventing an initial burst release of paclitaxel, enabling a more stable and controlled release over time. Additionally, Fe₃O₄ particles within the core facilitated magnetic targeting, allowing the capsules to be directed to specific areas within the body using an external magnetic field. Meanwhile, the GQDs provided fluorescence imaging capabilities, enabling the capsules to be tracked and visualized in biological systems. A notable feature of this drug delivery system was its responsiveness to ultrasound stimulation. The release of paclitaxel could be precisely triggered by ultrasound, with the release rate further adjustable by varying the duration of ultrasound exposure. These findings suggest that this innovative core–shell capsule system has significant potential for advancing controlled-release mechanisms and targeted drug delivery, especially for compounds that are typically challenging to administer due to their water-insolubility [66].

Some et al. demonstrated that GQDs loaded with curcumin exhibited significant anticancer activity in both laboratory and animal models [171]. The researchers developed three types of composites by combining curcumin with different graphene-based materials: graphene oxide (GO), double-layer graphene oxide (DGO), and GQDs, to enhance the delivery of the hydrophobic drug. A key factor in curcumin loading capacity was the interaction between curcumin and the oxygen-containing functional groups on GO, DGO, and GQDs. These functional groups, which vary with pH, played a crucial role in determining the efficiency of drug loading and the controlled release of curcumin [171]. The results showed that GQD-curcumin composites achieved the highest curcumin loading, reaching 40,800 mg/g, and provided the strongest anticancer effects compared to other curcumin-based composites with similar dosages [171]. Interestingly, fluorescence was absent in both free curcumin and the GQD-curcumin composites until curcumin was released from the GQDs. Upon curcumin's release, GQDs produced a detectable fluorescence signal, which could be used for tumor imaging along with drug release, effectively allowing GQDs to act as dual-function agents in cancer treatment by aiding both drug delivery and tumor visualization [172,173].

Iannazzoa et al. explored the application of GQDs in cancer treatment and drug delivery systems, finding that these specially prepared GQDs are highly effective at transporting drugs directly to cancer cells. Additionally, the study demonstrated the biocompatibility of these GQDs, meaning they can interact safely with biological systems. This approach allows for the integration of targeted ligand-based drugs within nanomaterials, which can be tailored for multifaceted treatments. Ultimately, this method could lead to improvements over traditional chemotherapy by reducing both toxicity and side effects, offering a more focused and safer treatment alternative. (Fig. 20 ) [174].

The unique physicochemical and biological characteristics of GQDs have made them promising tools in therapeutic applications [157]. These properties include the presence of diverse functional groups along the edges of GQDs, which can be chemically bonded to targeting ligands and therapeutic agents. Beyond just the edge groups, the structure of GQDs, specifically the presence of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms within their lattice, allows for therapeutic molecules-often containing aromatic rings themselves—to attach to the basal surfaces of GQDs. This feature significantly enhances the capacity for drug loading, supporting greater drug delivery and controlled release. Additionally, GQDs can amplify the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) when exposed to light, making them useful in photodynamic therapy (PDT). Fig. 21 provides a visual representation of the ways GQDs contribute to therapeutic applications. Multifunctional theranostic nanoparticles have emerged as promising tools to improve the effectiveness of current anticancer treatments. By selectively accumulating in cancerous tissues or cells, these nanoparticles not only enhance therapeutic efficacy but also reduce harmful side effects and minimize cytotoxic impacts on healthy tissues [166,175,176]. A major limitation of nanoparticles in targeting deeper tumor regions arises from their reliance on the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect, which allows for passive accumulation in solid tumors. However, this effect is strongly influenced by the size of the nanoparticles, impacting their penetration and distribution within tumors [176].

Functionalized graphene quantum dots (FGQDs), with their exceptionally small size and ability to traverse cellular membranes, offer significant potential to overcome limitations posed by tumor physiology. Their ability to enhance drug accumulation and permeability in targeted tissues makes them a promising option for cancer therapy [177]. Furthermore, GQDs offer additional benefits due to their inherent photoluminescent properties and high photothermal conversion efficiency when exposed to near-infrared (NIR) light. These characteristics position GQDs as optimal nanoplatforms for combined therapeutic and diagnostic (theranostic) applications, enhancing both the visualization and treatment of cancer. Thakur and colleagues developed near-infrared (NIR)-responsive GQDs using withered Ficus racemosa leaves as a carbon source [178]. These GQDs were designed as biocompatible nanomaterials suitable for in situ labeling of both healthy and cancerous cells. When exposed to an 808 nm laser, the GQDs exhibited a photothermal response that was dependent on their concentration, along with the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). Furthermore, confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) analysis showed that these GQDs retained their fluorescence even under continuous laser irradiation on cancer cells, confirming their stability and potential use in bioimaging applications [179].

Sui et al. investigated the potential of GQDs to enhance the anticancer effectiveness of cisplatin, a widely used chemotherapy drug. Cisplatin is known to be effective against various human cancers, including bladder, head and neck, lung, ovarian, and testicular cancers. However, its use is often associated with a range of serious side effects, such as kidney damage, gastrointestinal issues, reduced immune function, and the potential for developing drug resistance [180]. The study revealed that combining cisplatin with GQDs led to an enhanced cytotoxic impact on multiple cancer cell lines. Notably, this heightened toxicity was specific to cancer cells, as it did not occur in the normal cells tested [181]. The cisplatin/GQD complex was found to significantly increase cell cycle arrest in the G2/M phase and induce pronounced nuclear fragmentation and condensation in cancer cells compared to cisplatin alone. This increase in cellular uptake and enhanced toxicity of the drug was attributed to the small size of GQDs, which facilitates their entry into the cell nucleus, as well as their strong affinity for DNA. This affinity likely promotes an intensified interaction between cisplatin and DNA, improving the drug's efficacy against cancer cells. Several studies have documented the development of graphene quantum dot (GQD)-based delivery systems designed for the targeted release of the chemotherapy drug doxorubicin (DOX) into cancer cells. In these systems, DOX is loaded onto the surface of GQDs through strong pi-pi (π–π) stacking interactions. Additionally, targeting ligands are attached to the carboxylic acid groups present on the surface of the GQDs. This is achieved through covalent bonding facilitated by standard coupling agents, specifically 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) [179].

In a study by Wang et al., a targeted drug delivery system was developed using GQDs to specifically deliver doxorubicin (DOX), a widely used chemotherapy drug, to cancer cells [182]. The targeting of cancer cells was achieved by incorporating folic acid (FA) as a targeting ligand, due to its high affinity for receptors often overexpressed on the surface of cancer cells. To create this targeting system, the GQDs) were chemically bonded to FA by forming an amide bond, linking the amine group on FA with the carboxyl groups on the GQD surface. Doxorubicin (DOX) was then loaded onto the FA-conjugated GQDs through π-π stacking interactions. The study demonstrated that, even after drug loading, the FA-functionalized nanoparticles maintained their ability to specifically recognize and bind to cancer cells [183].
