This targeting capability was tested in vitro using HeLa cells, a widely used cancer cell line, where the nanosystem successfully entered the cells via receptor-mediated endocytosis. Once inside, the nanosystem released DOX directly within the targeted cells. Importantly, the biological tests showed that this delivery system exhibited significantly lower toxicity to non-cancerous "healthy" cells [183]. This selective cytotoxicity confirmed that the FA-functionalized GQDs could effectively distinguish between cancerous and non-cancerous cells, making it a promising approach for reducing side effects and enhancing the precision of cancer treatment [179,184]. Dong and colleagues enhanced GQDs by functionalizing them with poly (L-lactide) (PLA) and polyethylene glycol (PEG) to create a system capable of both intracellular miRNA imaging and gene delivery, ultimately improving therapeutic outcomes [185]. These modified GQDs, termed PLA-PEG-grafted-GQDs, exhibited high physiological stability and consistent photoluminescence properties. By conjugating PLA-PEG-grafted-GQDs with agents targeting miRNA-21 and the protein surviving, the researchers employed them as gene vectors specifically designed for delivery into HeLa cells. Inside the cells, the conjugated GQDs emitted green fluorescence, which was observable under a confocal microscope, allowing for enhanced visualization and more precise control of the gene delivery process. This approach proved effective for tracking and regulating therapeutic gene delivery within the cells [185].

GQDs demonstrate promising potential as sensing materials because of their ability to facilitate high-speed electron motion and reactions, making them excellent candidates for a wide range of sensing applications [186–192]. For instance, Sachin et al, after exploring the optimal condition, utilized SGQDs as a fluorescent probe for the detection of quercetin. As depicted in the PL spectra, the fluorescence emission peaked at a wavelength of 460 nm (λEm) with an excitation wavelength (λEx) of 360 nm. This prompted the investigation of the fluorescent probe sensing capabilities of SGQDs at different concentration of quercetin (0 to 50.0 μM), using λEx = 360 nm and λEm = 460 nm as excitation and emission wavelengths, respectively. Notably, a significant decrease in fluorescence emission of SGQDs with increasing concentration of quercetin. The Stern-Volmer plot between Fo/F and the concentration of quercetin, where Fo and F represent the initial and quenched fluorescence intensity, respectively. A strong linear relationship between the quenched fluorescence intensity and quercetin concentration was observed in the range of 0 to 50.0 μM with a regression coefficient (R2) of 0.9942. The LOD for quercetin was calculated using the equation LOD = 3σ/k (where k was the slope of the linear calibration curve and σ was the standard error for n = 10 replicate determinations of the blank SGQDs solution) and was estimated to be 0.006 μg/mL. Furthermore, the sensitivity of the fluorescent probe determined using the slope of linear curve was found to be 2.009 × 104 M-1, which was sufficient for quercetin sensing in real samples. The real-time image of SGQDs-Quercetin complex with varying concentrations (0–50.0 μM) of quercetin. The apparent change in color or increasing opacity of the SGQDs-Quercetin complex visually confirmed the presence of the quenching process. Additionally, this increasing opacity of the SGQDs-Quercetin complex was further quantified and confirmed through UV–vis absorption spectroscopy [193]. Chullasat et al., proposed a novel method for creating fluorescent sensors by combining inorganic QDs and GQDs. They synthesized a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) using APTES monomer, with cephalexin and ceftriaxone antibiotics as templates. Optical properties were imparted by cadmium-tellurium (CdTe) QDs and GQDs. Excitation at 355 nm resulted in maximum emission at 440 nm for GQDs and 575 nm for CdTe QDs, enabling concurrent determination of cephalexin with MIP-GQDs and ceftriaxone with MIP-CdTe QDs. The sensors exhibited good sensitivity and selectivity even in complex matrices like milk, showing promise for practical applications. Quenching of MIP-GQDs emission upon cephalexin addition demonstrated their effectiveness. This innovative approach offers highly sensitive and selective optosensors for antibiotic detection (Fig. 22 ) [194].

Ju et al.,[195]. conducted a study where they produced N-GQDs through a straightforward hydrothermal process involving CA and dicyandiamide. These N-GQDs displayed a notably high QY of 32.4% when excited at a wavelength of 350 nm. The researchers observed that these N-GQDs could serve as effective fluorescent probe for detecting glutathione (GSH). During the determination process, the intensity of N-GQDs PL spectra decreased upon interaction with Hg+ owing to the electron transfer and robust electrostatic interaction. However, upon the introduction of GSH, the PL intensity of N-GQDs rebounded as a result of preferred binding between Hg(II) and GSH, forming an Hg(II)-S bond. Under optimized conditions, this sensing system exhibited exceptional sensitivity and selectivity toward the detection of GSH, and achieving a LOD of 87 nM. Notably, the N-GQDs-Hg(II) system was effectively employed for imagining intra-cellular GSH in live HeLa cells owing to their improved luminescence, low-cytotoxicity, and biocompatibility. Additionally, the researchers developed a novel colorimetric sensor for enhanced GSH detection by utilizing the inherent peroxidase-like activity of AgNPs supported N-GQDs [196]. These nanocomposites facilitated the oxidation of 3, 3′ 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to oxTMB in the presence of H2O2, resulting in a visible color change. Since GSH may diminish oxTMB, the absorption intensity of oxTMB exhibited a strong correlation with the concentration of GSH. This active AgNPs-N-GQDs-TMB-H2O2 system demonstrated heightened sensitivity and selectivity for GSH detection, achieving a lower LOD of 31.0 nM and an extensive concentration range from 0.1 to157.6 μM. These findings suggest promising applications for the detection of GSH in blood serum samples and the potential for simple analytical methods in the future [159]. Mars et al described a method for detecting APOe4DNA (variant of apolipoprotein E gene) in human blood sample, which is associated with Alzheimer's disease (Fig. 23
A).

This approach involved the electrochemical and fluorescence-based incorporation of curcumin and GQDs onto an electrode coated with indium tin oxide (ITO). They then attached an amine-DNA probe to the electrode using EDC-NHS coupling chemistry, forming a complex with hybridized DNA and curcumin. By performing amperometric analysis, the researchers demonstrated that this technique could accurately quantify APOe4DNA within a linear range of 20-400 pg/mL, with a LOD of 0.48 pg/mL [137]. Zamir and co-workers pioneered the creation of an innovative sensor using poly-L-lysine (PLL)-GQDs for the purpose of detecting cysteine (cys) and homocysteine (hcys) (Fig. 23 B). They engineered PLL-GQDs fluorescent probe was fabricated by modifying the surface of GQDs with PLL, a biodegradable polycationic electrolyte. This modification aimed to enhance the sensor's sensitivity and specificity towards cys and hcys. The detection mechanism relied on the specific binding of cys and hcys molecules to PLL at the surfaces of PLL-GQDs, leading to dynamic quenching through a combination of electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions [197].

This review article delves into the expanding and increasingly promising prospects of FGQDs in a variety of biomedical applications. These include, but are not limited to, drug delivery, bioimaging, biosensing, and therapeutics. FGQDs have garnered significant attention due to their unique combination of exceptional physical properties such as high surface area, tunable optical characteristics, and remarkable electrical conductivity and their biocompatibility, which makes them ideal candidates for use in the biomedical field. The article underscores the critical importance of tuning the physical and chemical properties of FGQDs through strategic chemical modifications, which enable these nanomaterials to achieve optimal performance in a range of applications. For example, modifications to the surface chemistry of FGQDs can enhance their interactions with specific biomolecules, improve their stability in biological environments, and enable them to function effectively as sensors or carriers in drug delivery systems. Researchers have explored and developed a broad spectrum of innovative fabrication techniques to precisely tailor the properties of FGQDs, allowing for their customization to meet the diverse and evolving needs of biomedical applications. These techniques include various methods of functionalization, such as doping with heteroatoms, covalent attachment of targeting ligands, and non-covalent functionalization with biomolecules. These processes are designed to enhance the efficiency, selectivity, and biocompatibility of FGQDs in their intended application. For instance, the attachment of specific targeting molecules can facilitate the precise delivery of drugs to diseased tissues, while the functionalization with fluorescent probes enables high-resolution imaging for diagnostic purposes.

Notably, the integration of functional groups onto the surface of GQDs plays a pivotal role in improving their overall stability, biocompatibility, and functionality. By introducing various functional groups such as carboxyl, amine, or polyethylene glycol (PEG), the physicochemical properties of GQDs can be significantly altered, allowing for enhanced dispersion in biological systems, better resistance to agglomeration, and improved interaction with biological targets. These surface modifications not only increase the functional versatility of GQDs but also expand their potential applications across a wide range of biomedical fields, including personalized medicine, early disease detection, and therapeutic intervention. Through such innovations, FGQDs hold tremendous promise as multifunctional platforms for the next generation of biomedical technologies, with applications spanning diagnostics, targeted therapies, and regenerative medicine. Moreover, this review highlights the critical importance of comprehensive characterization techniques in understanding the structure–property relationships of FGQDs. Techniques such as SEM, TEM, AFM, XRD, FT-IR, UV-Vis, and PL are essential for providing detailed insights into the morphology, size, surface chemistry, and optical properties of FGQDs.

Despite the significant promise of FGQDs, several challenges persist that hinder their widespread commercialization. These include issues related to scalability, reproducibility, and long-term stability, which must be addressed to ensure consistent quality and performance in practical applications. Although considerable progress has been made in overcoming some of these obstacles, achieving cost-effective large-scale production, ensuring batch-to-batch uniformity, and maintaining the long-term stability of FGQDs remain critical hurdles. These challenges must be tackled before FGQDs can be seamlessly integrated into real-world industrial and clinical settings. Interdisciplinary collaborations across fields such as materials science, nanotechnology, biology, and engineering are expected to play a pivotal role in accelerating the development of innovative FGQD-based materials and technologies. Such collaborations could lead to novel applications across diverse sectors, unlocking the full potential of FGQDs. In this context, FGQDs hold significant promise within the broader realm of carbon-based materials, with the potential to drive advancements in environmentally friendly technologies. While the prospects for FGQDs are undoubtedly exciting, it is important to temper expectations. Their full effectiveness can only be fully assessed once devices incorporating FGQDs are rigorously engineered, tested, and widely adopted across various industries. Nevertheless, their unique properties offer a glimmer of optimism for a future where eco-conscious, molecular-based devices become commonplace, particularly in fields such as clean energy, healthcare, and environmental remediation. It is also crucial to acknowledge that research on GQDs is still in its early stages, with vast untapped potential for both computational and experimental exploration. This nascent phase of research leaves considerable room for further advancements, especially in the precise control of their size, surface functionalization, and overall performance, which are key to realizing their full potential.
Data will be made available on request.
